title: The practice of letting people see you without your mask
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
What we call the mask

I spent decades perfecting mine. You probably did too.

The mask isn't just a social smile or a polite "I'm fine" when you're clearly not. It's a whole survival system. Your voice changes. Your posture shifts. You learn exactly which facial expressions are acceptable and which ones get you in trouble. You learn to laugh at the right moments. You learn to look interested when you're dissociating through another conversation about the weather.

Here's the thing. The mask kept you alive.

I mean that literally. For those of us with complex trauma, the mask wasn't vanity or social awkwardness. It was protection. If you grew up in a home where your real emotions were dangerous, where your authentic reactions got you punished or shamed or ignored, you learned to put on a face that would keep you safe. That's not weakness. That's genius-level survival adaptation.

Peter Levine, the guy who literally wrote the book on somatic experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. Your nervous system learned that certain expressions, certain tones of voice, certain ways of holding yourself were safe. Everything else was a risk. So you automated the safe version. You became a pro at being the person people expected you to be.

But here's what nobody tells you. The mask doesn't just protect you from others. It protects you from yourself.

When you've been wearing it for years, decades, you stop knowing what's underneath. The mask becomes your face. The performance becomes your identity. And you start to believe that if anyone saw the real you, the messy, angry, scared, needy, complicated you, they would leave. They would recoil. They would confirm what you've always suspected. That you're too much. Or not enough. Or broken.

That belief is the trauma talking. But it feels like the truest thing in the world.

Why showing your face feels like dying

I remember the first time I tried to let someone see me without the mask. I was twenty-seven. My hands were shaking. My throat felt like it was closing. I thought I was going to pass out.

And all I did was say "I'm actually not okay."

Wild, right? Such a simple sentence. But my nervous system treated it like a life-or-death situation. Because in a way, it was. My brain had learned, through years of evidence, that being seen meant being in danger. Showing vulnerability meant giving someone ammunition. Being authentic meant risking abandonment.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, would say my ventral vagal system was offline. That's the part of your nervous system that allows you to feel safe in connection. When you grow up in an unpredictable or threatening environment, that system doesn't develop properly. Your body stays in defense mode. And defense mode says: keep the mask on, scan for threats, don't let them see you.

So when I try to take the mask off, my body screams no. It floods with cortisol. My heart races. I want to run. I want to laugh it off and change the subject. I want to say "just kidding" and retreat back into the character I've been playing.

That's not failure. That's your nervous system doing its job. It's trying to protect you from a threat it still believes is real.

The problem is, the threat might not be real anymore. The people who hurt you, who taught you that your real self was unacceptable, they might be long gone. Or they might still be in your life but no longer have the same power over you. But your body doesn't know that. Your body is still living in 1997, or 2003, or whenever the danger was real.

Taking the mask off means teaching your body a new truth. And that takes time. A lot of time. More time than any wellness influencer wants to admit.

The slow work of being seen

Let me be honest with you. I have not arrived at some place of perfect unmasked living. I still put the mask on. Sometimes I need to. Sometimes I choose to because it's easier or safer or because I don't have the energy to be real with someone who won't get it.

But I've learned something important. The practice isn't about never wearing the mask. It's about knowing you're wearing it. It's about having the choice.

For me, the practice started small. Like, embarrassingly small.

I started by telling one person one thing I usually hid. I told my therapist that I was scared of her judging me. That was it. That was the big revelation. And she didn't judge me. She just nodded and said "of course you are" and we kept talking.

That moment didn't fix everything. But it planted a seed. My nervous system got a tiny piece of evidence that maybe, just maybe, being seen wasn't lethal.

From there, I started experimenting. I told a friend I was having a hard day without following it up with a joke. I let someone see me cry without apologizing. I admitted I didn't know something instead of pretending I did. I said "I need help" instead of "I've got this."

Each time, my body went through the same panic. Each time, I survived. Each time, I collected another piece of evidence that contradicted the old story.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance by Tara Brach. She talks about this exact thing. About how we can hold our mask-wearing with compassion instead of judgment. About how we can learn to say "yes, I'm wearing a mask right now, and that's okay" without using it as an excuse to stay hidden forever.

Stay with me here. The goal isn't to rip the mask off dramatically. That's usually retraumatizing. The goal is to slowly, carefully, at your own pace, let more of your real self be seen by people who have earned the right to see it.

And you get to choose who those people are. Not everyone gets access to the unmasked you. Not everyone deserves it. Part of healing is learning to discriminate between safe and unsafe people, instead of treating everyone as dangerous or everyone as safe.

What happens when the mask slips

Here's something nobody warns you about. When you start letting people see you, some of them will react badly.

Not because you're doing something wrong. But because your mask was serving them too. Your family system, your friend group, your workplace, they all got comfortable with the version of you that didn't make waves. The version that was agreeable and easy and never asked for anything. When you start showing your real feelings, your real needs, your real boundaries, people who benefited from your mask will try to put it back on you.

They'll say you've changed. They'll say you're too sensitive. They'll say you used to be so easy to be around.

And that hurts. It really hurts. Because the part of you that still wants to be loved and accepted will hear those words and want to retreat back into the safety of the mask.

But here's what I've learned. The people who love you for your mask don't actually love you. They love the character you've been playing. And if you want real connection, real love, real belonging, you have to be willing to lose the people who only wanted the performance.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored by Deb Dana. She's a polyvagal expert who breaks down how to actually shift your nervous system toward safety. Because the mask isn't just a psychological habit. It's a physiological state. And you can't think your way out of it. You have to work with your body.

I also found a lot of validation in understanding why I developed the mask in the first place. A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson. It helped me see that my mask wasn't a personal failing. It was a perfectly reasonable response to parents who couldn't handle my real emotions. That shifted something for me. The shame started to loosen.

The unmasked life is not what you think

I used to think that taking the mask off meant I would finally be happy. That all my problems would dissolve. That I would walk through life in a state of radiant authenticity, glowing with self-acceptance, never faking another smile again.

That's not what happened.

What happened was messier. I cried more. I got angry more. I had to have hard conversations I had been avoiding for years. I lost relationships. I grieved the person I had been pretending to be. I grieved the safety of the mask, even though it was a prison.

But here's what also happened. I started to feel things in my body that I hadn't felt since I was a child. Joy, real joy, not the performative kind. Sadness that moved through me instead of getting stuck. Connection that felt alive instead of scripted.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook by Arielle Schwartz. It walks through the actual steps of reclaiming parts of yourself that got hidden behind the mask. It's not about becoming someone new. It's about coming home to who you always were.

Think about it. The mask isn't a false self. It's a real self, a survival self, a self that did important work to keep you alive. That self deserves gratitude, not rejection. But that self also deserves to rest. To know that it's safe now. To let other parts of you come forward.

I don't know what your unmasked face looks like. I don't know what you'll find when you start letting people see you. Maybe you'll find rage that's been buried for decades. Maybe you'll find a tenderness you've never let anyone touch. Maybe you'll find a goofy, playful part of yourself that got locked away because it wasn't safe to be silly in your house.

I can't promise you'll like what you find. I can't promise it will be easy. What I can promise is that it will be real. And real, as it turns out, is worth the terror.

One last thing. You don't have to do this alone. The myth of the solitary healer, the lone warrior who faces their demons in isolation, that's another kind of mask. Real healing happens in relationship. With a therapist, a support group, a trusted friend, a partner who earns your trust over years. You need witnesses. You need people who can look at you, really look at you, and not flinch.

Find those people. Let them see you. Start small. Start with one sentence. Start with one moment where you tell the truth about how you're feeling.

Your nervous system will scream. That's okay. Let it scream. And then let it learn, one tiny piece of evidence at a time, that being seen is not the same as being destroyed.

It might be the beginning of being free.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance by Tara Brach - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • Anchored by Deb Dana - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook by Arielle Schwartz - A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson - A book that many find validates their childhood experience
